titleInfo.title: An Investigation into Early Buddhism in Sichuan

mods.abstract: 1.It is generally assumed that Buddhism entered the Central Plains of China via the Western Regions, and from there came to Sichuan via Guangyuan, proceeding to Dazu. This is called the northern route to Sichuan, but there might have been other routes as well. The earliest Buddhist site in Sichuan is the Tiancheng cave 天成窟at Qianfoya 千佛崖 in Guangyuan 广元. The bodhisattva figures there resemble the sculpture of the Wei dynasty at Maijishan 麦积山 and Gongxian 巩县. Some scholars believe that Buddhist art entered Shaanxi through the Hexi corridor, and was transmitted from Shaanxi to Guangyuan first, then to other places in Sichuan.Recent excavations in Chengdu (商业街，西安路) have unearthedearly Buddhist sculptures with a style very different from those sculptures of Guangyuan that were introduced from the north. A Maitreya Buddha from Xi’an lu resembles in style paintings from the Southern Dynasties in the style of 秀骨清象 (elegant bones, clear appearance). The sculptures from the Southern Dynasties discovered in Chengdu are also not entirely alike the contemporaneous sculpture from the Central Plains. 2. There are many more differneces between the Chendu sculptures excavated in Xi’an Lu and Luoyang sculpture. The iconographic motives are more diversified in Chengdu, including Maitrya, Amitayus, Śākyamuni and Prabhūtaratna, and Three Buddhas not referring to the three times, but to the three directions or trikaya. In fact, when the Northern Wei dynasty transferred the capital to Luoyang in 494, one reason for doing so was to be closer to Han culture, and the model of this culture was held to be the Chinese South. 3. Before Northern Buddhism entered Sichuan via Guangyuan, Buddhism was already flourishing in Sichuan. 4. Even though there is no doubt that the Buddhist teaching reached China via Central Asia and the Silk Road, since ancient times, at least two more routes connected India with China: The southeastern sea route, and the southwestern Sichuan - India route (蜀 - 身毒道). The southwestern route existed already before the Han dynasty, maybe even in the 5th century BCE, and connected ancient India with the states of Chu 楚, Wu 吳, and Shu 蜀, from today’s Sichuan to Yunnan and then to India. Under the Qin 秦 dynasty, already two roads existed between Sichuan and Yunnan, the Bo 僰 route, and the Qingyi 青衣道 route. The sea route betweenWu and India already existed before the Han dynasty, and the connection between Sanxia 三峽 (east of Chengdu) and Chu/Wu can be traced back to the late Neolithic. Monk Faxian 法顯, who travelled to India in 399, returned on the searoute in 412, when he landed in Shandong and made his way from there to Jiankang 健康 (today’s Nanjing). It can be assumed that Buddhism had already reached Sichuan from the east or south before it was introduced via the northern route. 5. Lei Yuhau goes on to list objects with early Buddhist representations (stone sculpture, money trees, ceramics, painted bricks) in Sichuan. Leshan has to be taken as the eastermost point of the southwestern India - Sichuan route. But also in neighboring Hubei in Ezhou 鄂州 (the ancient city of Wuchang 武昌城) Buddhsit motivs are found on bronze mirrors. From here, Buddhism spread into Jiangsu and Zhejiang, as excavations of tombs from the Six Dynasties have shown. From that time, no Buddhist artefacts are found in the north of China. Therefore it can be assumed that from the end of the Eastern Han to the Jin dynasty, Buddhism flourished first in the western parts of Sichuan, Hubei, Jiangsu and Zhejiang, and developed its own characteristics.The art of cave temples and its related styles were introduced later from the north.
